summary: Jeff Atwood announces a CodeProject article about converting URLs to MHTML archives using .NET. The utility can save web pages in four formats (complete HTML with files, single MHTML archive, HTML only, or text), mimicking Internet Explorer's 'Save As' functionality. He explains the practical use case of needing single-file web archives for emailing reports or storing in document management systems. Atwood expresses surprise that IE's web archive format is actually based on an internet standard (RFC 2557).
key_points: The .NET class can convert any URL to four different formats in a single line of code
MHTML archives are useful for emailing reports or storing in document management systems because they contain everything in one file
IE's 'Web Archive' format is based on RFC 2557, an actual internet standard for multipart MIME messages
The functionality also works in Firefox through an extension
money_quotes: It's a lot easier to email someone a report – or store it in a document management system – when you have a single MHTML file!
I was as surprised as anyone else to find that IE's 'Web Archive' save option is based on an actual internet standard
key_insight: Single-file web archives based on the MHTML standard solve the practical problem of sharing complete web pages without losing embedded resources.
